publications.title: A molecular basis for the control of preimmune escape variants by HIV-specific CD8+ T cells.
publications.authors: Ladell K, Hashimoto M, Iglesias MC, Wilmann PG, McLaren JE, Gras S, Chikata T, Kuse N, Fastenackels S, Gostick E, Bridgeman JS, Venturi V, Arkoub ZA, Agut H, van Bockel DJ, Almeida JR, Douek DC, Meyer L, Venet A, Takiguchi M, Rossjohn J, Price DA, Appay V.
publications.executive_summary: This study elucidates how HIV-specific CD8+ T cells maintain control over rapidly mutating virus by examining the immunodominant KK10 epitope (KRWIILGLNK) from HIV-1 p24 Gag presented on HLA-B∗2705. The researchers identified cross-reactive TCR clonotypes that recognize both wild-type and escape-variant forms of KK10 with equivalent binding affinity and nearly identical docking geometries, explaining the broad antiviral activity observed in infected individuals. This structural insight reveals a key mechanism of adaptive immunity: the pre-existing TCR repertoire can accommodate TCR-accessible mutations in viral epitopes, thereby constraining viral escape evolution and forcing the virus to develop more drastic variants that evade antigen presentation altogether rather than TCR recognition. The work is significant for understanding how the immune system adapts to rapidly evolving pathogens and has implications for rational TCR or vaccine design strategies targeting viral escape.
